title: CONTRIBUTING.md: add section about describing changes
body:
Add a section about describing changes in commit messages.  GitHub tends
to drive the large part of discussions and change descriptions to the
corresponding pull requests and issues, but such information is lost in
the git history.  Not providing sufficient information in commit
messages is painful for reviewing and can cause issues while debugging.
It also complicates studying source code, where reading commit messages
and the code's git history is a common approach to better understand the
code.

Following the descriptions should be enforced by the maintainers of the
libpod project.  Pull requests containing commits without proper
descriptions should not be merged.

This change bases on the documentation of the Linux kernel v4.17:
https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/v4.17/process/submitting-patches.html

Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <vrothberg@suse.com>

comments.body:
I think we should follow the Kernel community and enforce proper descriptions of commit messages. `git log` shows a very bad state of our git history and I think we should improve the situation in the future. This change may help new contributors and may draw some attention to the problem.

@baude @mheon @umohnani8 @TomSweeneyRedHat @giuseppe @rhatdan (sorry if I miss someone)

> I think we should follow the Kernel community and enforce proper descriptions of commit messages. `git log` shows a very bad state of our git history and I think we should improve the situation in the future. This change may help new contributors and may draw some attention to the problem.

I fully support this.  Sometimes I try to catch bad commits in reviews.  This is not only aesthetic, it makes impossible to use tools like `git bisect` and `git blame`.

I'd add that every patch must at least build, what do you think?
I'm fine with this.  (Although I don't always follow it as I should)

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

I'm with @rhatdan on this one - I like the change, but I'm occasionally one of the people letting in bad commit messages and overly complex commits. I'll try to improve that from here on out.

Wording changes LGTM, it's a worthwhile goal to work towards.
LGTM, probably ought to add the same to Buildah.
Thanks! I corrected it from "kernel" to "libpod repository"

> LGTM, probably ought to add the same to Buildah.

Maybe, we can create common documents for all `github.com/containers` projects?
Common docs would be great if we could manage it.

> Common docs would be great if we could manage it.

Any idea how we can achieve this on GitHub? (Cc @rhatdan) I imagine that all projects under the `containers` umbrella have some pull-request template pointing to those docs that explain all things.

@vrothberg I'm not sure how to make that happen in GitHub and then to have links like "Contributing" in libpod point to it rather than the local CONTRIBUTING.md file.  I'll see if I can nose around a bit.

Common would be great.  No idea how to do it.
/lgtm

I poked about GitHub docs today, didn't find any bread crumbs.  I sent a help request to GitHub support asking if it's possible to put a file into an organization and used it from 1 to n projects, we'll see what they have to say.  We could potentially setup a project for common stuff and then make our projects a sub-project underneath it, but Google didn't show much positive feedback for that kind of arrangement.  In fact most comments said to avoid it at all possible costs.

Thanks a lot, @TomSweeneyRedHat! I think that creating a new project under `github.com/containers` that contains all such shared documents is a way to approach it. The PR templates can then link to the markdown files.
